sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 63, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 39 or fewer. You are 1.6x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 31 actions returned the board to a state already seen 31 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 31 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over (timer ran out). The game has reset. Let me look at the current state to see where the blue rectangle is now. The game_over flag is set, which means the timer ran out. The game will reset and I need to try a new approach.
Let me look at the current frame to see the reset state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== CURRENT STATE (after game over) ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  {n['color']} id={n['id']} hash={n['hash']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game has reset. The blue rectangle is back at the starting position (rows 16-19, cols 12-31). The timer is back to full (64 green cells).
Now I need to completely rethink my approach. I've been exploring for 62 actions without completing the level. Let me think about what I haven't tried.
Key observations:
1. Blue rectangle moves with arrow keys (4 cells per press)
2. Blue rectangle vertical range: rows 16-44
3. Blue rectangle horizontal range: cols 0-55 (approximately)
4. Two yellow U-shaped containers at bottom
5. Charcoal + magenta blocks at top right (cols 36-39)
6. Timer bar at top (green, counts down by 2 per action)
7. SPACE does nothing (or at least, I haven't seen it do anything)
8. MOUSE clicks on various objects do nothing visible
I've tried moving the blue rectangle to many positions without success. I've tried SPACE and MOUSE without success.
Let me think about what the goal could be. The charcoal block is at cols 36-39 (4 cols wide) and the magenta block is also at cols 36-39 (4 cols wide). Together they form a 7-row tall column at cols 36-39.
What if the charcoal/magenta blocks represent a "goal marker" that indicates where the blue rectangle should END UP? The blue rectangle is 20 cols wide, so it can't fit exactly at cols 36-39. But maybe the goal is to position the blue rectangle so that its CENTER aligns with the charcoal/magenta column's CENTER?
The charcoal/magenta column is at cols 36-39 (center at col 37.5). For the blue rectangle (20 cols) to be centered at col 37.5, it needs to be at cols 28-47.
From the starting position (cols 12-31), that's 16 cols right = 4 RIGHT presses.
But I already tried this position and the level didn't complete. Or did I? Let me check...
Actually, I'm not sure I tried this exact position. Let me try it now, but this time I'll also try pressing SPACE after moving to that position.
Wait, but I've already tried SPACE at various positions without success. Let me think about what else I could try.
Actually, let me reconsider the game. What if the goal is NOT to move the blue rectangle to a specific position, but rather to MOVE THE CHARCOAL/MAGENTA BLOCKS? But I don't have any action that moves those blocks.
Or what if the goal is to CHANGE THE COLOR of the blue rectangle? But I don't have any action that changes colors.
Let me try a completely different approach. What if the game is about the blue rectangle's POSITION relative to the yellow containers? The left container is at cols 16-27 and the right container is at cols 40-51. The blue rectangle starts at cols 12-31, which is centered above the LEFT container.
What if the goal is to move the blue rectangle to a position where it's centered above the RIGHT container? The right container is at cols 40-51 (center at col 45.5). For the blue rectangle (20 cols) to be centered at col 45.5, it needs to be at cols 36-55.
From the starting position (cols 12-31), that's 24 cols right = 6 RIGHT presses.
Let me try this: move RIGHT 6 times and see if the level completes.
But wait, I already tried moving the blue rectangle to the right and the level didn't complete. Let me check the history...
Actually, I did try moving RIGHT multiple times, and the blue rectangle ended up at cols 36-55. But the level didn't complete. So maybe the goal is NOT to move the blue rectangle to the right container.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. What if the goal involves the blue rectangle's VERTICAL position? The blue rectangle starts at rows 16-19. What if I need to move it DOWN to a specific row?
The yellow containers are at rows 52-59. The blue rectangle can move from rows 16-44. So the blue rectangle can get close to the containers but can't overlap with them.
What if the goal is to move the blue rectangle to the LOWEST possible position (rows 44-47) and then to a specific horizontal position?
Actually, let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the game is about the blue rectangle's COLOR matching the color of one of the yellow containers? The blue rectangle is blue (b) and the containers are yellow (Y). What if I need to change the blue rectangle's color to yellow?
But I don't have any action that changes colors. Unless... clicking on the blue rectangle with MOUSE changes its color? Let me try that.
Actually, I already tried clicking on the blue rectangle and nothing happened. So that's not it.
OK, let me try yet another hypothesis. What if the game is about the blue rectangle's SHAPE? The blue rectangle is 20x4. What if I need to change its shape?
But I don't have any action that changes shapes. Unless... pressing SPACE changes the shape? Let me try pressing SPACE at the starting position.
Actually, I already tried SPACE and nothing happened. So that's not it either.
I'm really stuck. Let me try one more thing: what if the game is about the blue rectangle's POSITION relative to the CHARCOAL/MAGENTA blocks? The charcoal block is at rows 1-3 and the magenta at rows 4-7. What if I need to move the blue rectangle to a position where it's directly BELOW the magenta block?
The magenta block is at rows 4-7, cols 36-39. Directly below would be rows 8-11, cols 36-39. But the blue rectangle can only move from rows 16-44, so it can't reach rows 8-11.
Wait, but what if the blue rectangle CAN move to rows 8-11? Let me check: from the starting position (rows 16-19), I moved UP and the blue rectangle went to rows 12-15, then 8-11. But I didn't try going further up.
Actually, I DID try moving UP multiple times and the blue rectangle stopped at rows 16-19. So the top limit is row 16.
Hmm, wait. Let me re-examine this. When I moved UP from the starting position (rows 16-19), the blue rectangle went to rows 12-15, then 8-11. But then I moved DOWN and it went back to rows 16-19. So the blue rectangle CAN move to rows 8-11, but it stops at rows 16-19 when moving UP from the starting position?
No, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine the history...
Actually, I think I made an error. Let me check the history more carefully. When I moved UP from the starting position, the blue rectangle went to rows 12-15. Then I moved UP again and it went to rows 8-11. Then I moved UP again and... what happened?
Let me check the history to see the exact sequence of positions.
Actually, I don't have easy access to the full history of positions. Let me just try moving the blue rectangle UP from the starting position and see where it goes.
Wait, the game just reset. The blue rectangle is at rows 16-19, cols 12-31. Let me try moving UP and see where it goes.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the current state and try moving UP from start
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"Blue start: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Try moving UP to see the full vertical range
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
